name: Switzerland
summary: Home to world-leading study value (ETH Zürich/EPFL, top-10 CS, at ~CHF 1,460/yr tuition), the world's highest tech salaries (Google Zürich median ~CHF 292k), low tax, a top-3 passport and dual citizenship retained both ways — but the immigration PATH is the obstacle for this profile: only a 6-month post-study permit plus annual non-EU work quotas, ~10 years to a C-permit (PR) and a 10-year, multi-level, discretionary naturalisation, alongside the 2009 minaret ban, a domestic halal-slaughter ban and a small Bangladeshi community. Derived overall sits below the 60% inclusion line.

job-market.summary: A premier AI hub (ETH, Google Zürich, IBM Research) with the world's highest salaries and an acute IT/AI shortage (~117k specialists needed by 2028), so a well-placed graduate finds deep demand — though non-EU hiring is constrained by annual work-permit quotas and a labour-market test.

pros.text: A top global AI hub (ETH Zürich, Google Zürich, IBM Research) with the world's highest tech pay: Zürich software engineers ~CHF 112k–215k, AI/ML specialists ~CHF 167k–247k, Google Zürich median ~CHF 292k.

cons.text: Non-EU/EFTA hiring is capped by annual work-permit quotas and requires a labour-market test (employer must show no suitable EU candidate), a real barrier for Bangladeshi graduates.
German/French is commonly expected outside ETH/global-firm roles.

links.title: Levels.fyi — Google Software Engineer salary, Greater Zürich

visa-access.summary: A Swiss embassy in Dhaka and cantonal student approval that is reliable for genuine ETH/EPFL-admitted students, but processing is slow and the non-EU framework is quota-bound; living-cost funds proof is high.

pros.text: Switzerland has an embassy in Dhaka; student visas (via cantonal approval) are reliable for genuine admitted students, and ETH/EPFL admission carries weight.

cons.text: Cantonal processing is slow, funds proof for high-cost Switzerland is significant, and the broader non-EU framework is quota-managed.

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is top-3, but naturalisation is among the hardest anywhere — 10 years' residence plus cantonal/communal residence, a national language and a multi-level discretionary review.

pros.text: Switzerland permits dual citizenship without restriction (Bangladesh's 'all of Europe' DNC scope applies), so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
The Swiss passport ranks joint 3rd on Henley 2026 (~186 destinations).

cons.text: Ordinary naturalisation needs 10 years' federal residence plus cantonal/communal residence (2–5 yrs), a C permit, a national language (B1 oral/A2 written), integration, financial independence and a clean record.
Decisions move through commune → canton → confederation with real local discretion (some communes vote), making the outcome uncertain even after 10 years.

post-study-work.summary: The weakest PSW in the set: only a 6-month, non-renewable job-search permit (15 hrs/week), and conversion to work is quota-bound by a labour-market test.

pros.text: Graduates of a Swiss university can get a 6-month residence permit to find work (15 hrs/week allowed during the search); each spouse qualifies.

cons.text: The permit is only 6 months and non-renewable — the shortest runway of any country here.
Converting to a skilled-work permit is capped by non-EU quotas and a labour-market test.

links.title: ETH Zürich — residence permit for job-seeking after graduation (official)

spouse-family.summary: Family reunification with spouse work rights and free schooling, though subject to permit type and quota; if both spouses study, each holds an independent track.

pros.text: Family members of a B-permit holder can join and work, children attend public school free, and if both spouses study each holds their own permit.

cons.text: Reunification and spouse work rights depend on permit type and income, and the non-EU quota constrains the independent route.

msc-study.summary: A standout: ETH Zürich and EPFL (top-10 global CS/AI) at extraordinarily low tuition (~CHF 1,460/yr) — the best quality-to-cost study option in the set.

pros.text: ETH Zürich and EPFL are top-10 global for CS/AI with English-taught master's at only ~CHF 1,460/yr — world-class quality at near-free public tuition.

cons.text: Admission is highly competitive, student work is capped at 15 hrs/week, and the downstream 6-month PSW limits the study-to-work bridge.

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence (C permit) for non-EU nationals generally takes ~10 years, one of the longest routes in the set.

pros.text: Once a C permit is obtained, status is secure and stable in a predictable system.

cons.text: Non-EU nationals generally need ~10 years of residence for the C permit (PR), one of the longest routes here, with integration and language conditions.

income-cost.summary: The world's highest salaries plus low tax yield an exceptional surplus, even against Switzerland's very high living and housing costs.

pros.text: The combination of the world's highest tech salaries and low Swiss income tax produces an exceptional net surplus and remittance capacity — even after high living costs.
Near-free ETH/EPFL tuition keeps the study-phase fee burden low.

cons.text: Living and especially housing costs in Zürich/Geneva are among the world's highest, and mandatory health-insurance premiums add to fixed costs.

healthcare.summary: World-class care via mandatory private insurance — excellent quality and short waits, but high monthly premiums and deductibles.

pros.text: World-class healthcare with short waits; all residents are covered via mandatory insurance taken out within 3 months of arrival.

cons.text: Premiums are high (~CHF 300+/month per adult) with deductibles, and childcare is very expensive.

culture-language.summary: English works in Zürich's tech/finance and at ETH, but German/French/Italian is needed for daily life and status, and the climate is reserved with a notable anti-Islam vote history.

pros.text: English works well in Zürich's tech/finance world and at ETH/EPFL, and quality of life/livability is among the world's highest.

cons.text: A Swiss national language (German/French/Italian) is needed for daily life and citizenship, and the society is reserved with a documented anti-Islam vote history (minaret ban).

safety-law.summary: Among the world's safest with top-tier rule of law, tempered by the constitutional minaret ban as an anti-discrimination carve-out.

pros.text: Very low crime, top-tier rule of law and reliable institutions in a wealthy, stable direct democracy with strong women's safety.

cons.text: The 2009 constitutional minaret ban is an entrenched anti-discrimination carve-out targeting Muslim religious expression.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: A very stable direct democracy, but the SVP (largest party) drives restrictive, quota-based migration and an anti-Islam streak (the minaret ban came via referendum).

pros.text: Institutions are exceptionally stable (consensus government, direct democracy), and skilled-talent recruitment for global firms continues.

cons.text: The SVP (largest party) drives quota-based restriction of non-EU migration, and direct-democracy votes have produced anti-Islam outcomes (the 2009 minaret ban).

links.title: SWI swissinfo — the minaret ban, ten years on

tax.summary: A low overall tax burden (canton-dependent, ~20–30% effective) on the world's highest salaries; no special expat regime needed, with a Switzerland–Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: Switzerland has a low overall tax burden (canton-dependent, ~20–30% effective) on the world's highest salaries, and a Switzerland–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: Joint spousal assessment can penalise the second earner, and there is a wealth tax; no special expat regime exists (though base rates are already low).

muslim-diaspora.summary: A ~5–6% Muslim population (mostly Balkan/Turkish) with mosques and imported halal, but constrained by the minaret ban, a domestic halal-slaughter ban and a small Bangladeshi community.

pros.text: Muslims number ~450,000 (~5–6%) with established mosques and prayer spaces in cities, and imported halal meat is legally available.

cons.text: New minarets are banned (2009 constitutional vote), domestic halal (ritual) slaughter is prohibited, and the Bangladeshi-specific community and South-Asian ecosystem are small.

links.title: US State Dept — 2023 Religious Freedom Report: Switzerland

direct-work-route.summary: World-class demand and pay, but a structurally hard door for a non-EU applicant. As a third-country national a Bangladeshi competes for a fixed annual quota (4,500 B + 4,000 L permits for all of Switzerland, frozen for 2026) and the employer must pass a labour-market test (priority to Swiss/EU/EFTA, documented RAV/EURES advertising) plus an economic-interest test — admission is limited to 'managers, specialists and qualified workers' (university degree + several years' experience), so a 2022 BSc-only profile sits at the lower edge. Salaries are high (Zürich SWE ~CHF 112–137k; far more at Google/IBM Research) and AI/IT demand is acute, but quota + test make non-EU direct hire selective, not routine. The onward chain is sound but long: C permit (PR) generally ~10 years for non-EU, citizenship ~10 years with cantonal/communal discretion; dual citizenship is allowed (Switzerland within Bangladesh's all-Europe DNC scope).

pros.text: Acute IT/AI shortage (~117,000 additional specialists needed by 2028) and the world's highest tech salaries — strong pull for scarce/senior profiles; dual citizenship retained.
Once a B permit is secured the onward chain is well-trodden (B → C → citizenship), and both spouses can progress in parallel.

cons.text: Third-country direct hire is gated by a fixed annual quota (4,500 B + 4,000 L for all of Switzerland, frozen for 2026), a mandatory labour-market test (priority to Swiss/EU/EFTA) and an economic-interest test — selective, not routine, especially for a junior BSc profile.
Negligible BD track record (~3,000 community); PR (C permit) and citizenship each ~10 years for non-EU; direction flat-to-tightening (SVP 10%-cut initiative, Oct-2026 elections).

Third-country quotas for 2026 left unchanged — Federal Council (official)
